answer:
**The work is *Manusmṛti* (also known as the *Laws of Manu*), Chapter 1.**

This chapter opens with a cosmogony matching your description precisely. The divine Self-Existent One (Svayambhu) creates waters, deposits his seed therein, which becomes a golden egg. He is reborn within it as Brahma, dwells a year, then splits it to form heaven and earth (among other things). Later in the same creation sequence, he produces the four *varnas* (social orders)—Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya, and Shudra—from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

Here are the verbatim verses (in translation):

> That seed became a **golden egg**, in splendor like the thousand-rayed sun; in it he himself was born as Brahma, the great father of all the worlds. // Mn_1.9 //[^1]
>
> After dwelling in that **egg** for a full year, that divine Lord, by his own contemplation, **split the egg in two**. // Mn_1.12 //[^2]
>
> And with those two halves he formed the **heavens and the earth**, and between them the middle region, the eight directions, and the eternal abode of the waters. // Mn_1.13 //[^3]
>
> And for the sake of the prosperity of the worlds, he caused the **Brahmana, the Kshatriya, the Vaishya, and the Shudra** to emanate from his **mouth, arms, thighs, and feet**. // Mn_1.31 //[^4]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-153021).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-cd6713).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-3bd729).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-13a2db).
